retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of the pigmentation is generally good, but extra-dermal manifestations of the pigmentation may exacerbate it. For example, hydroxychloroquine may cause pigmentation of the oral mucosa, and minocycline can cause pigmentation of heart valves. [8] [9] Otherwise, drug-induced pigmentation does not correlate with increased mortality. There may be psychological or social impacts depending upon the individual patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of drug-induced pigmentation is thought to involve several different mechanisms. It could be due to the accumulation of melanin (e.g., antimalarials), either by a direct trigger of the medication or nonspecific inflammation caused by the drug. This form of pigmentation is worsened by sun exposure, explaining patients’ worsened pigmentation in sunny areas. Additionally, the drug itself can accumulate and cause pigmentation. The drug can remain within dermal macrophages and even undergo chemical changes to newer types of particles, as exhibited by gold complexes. Finally, the last two mechanisms for drugs that can cause pigmentation involves the synthesis of new pigment (lipofuscin) or accumulation of iron (minocycline). [4] The latter is thought to involve damaged blood vessels and lysis of red blood cells. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating whether a patient has pigmentation related to medication use, it is crucial to take into consideration several points. First, it is vital to take a thorough medical history of the patient, which involves noting all the medications the patient is taking and carefully evaluating any that have pigmentation-related side effects. Common drugs that are known to cause pigmentation include NSAIDS, antimalarials, amiodarone, anticoagulants, antimicrobials, antiretrovirals, and tetracyclines. Additionally, a provider should note when the pigmentation starts and if there are any changes, such as increased or decreased intensity, after altering the usage of the drug. For example, amiodarone-induced pigmentation exhibits a dose-dependent relationship in regards to its appearance. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced pigmentation represents a complex issue for patients who are prescribed certain medications. These medications may include NSAIDs, tetracyclines, antimalarials, heavy metals, etc. This activity reviews the etiology, epidemiology, pathophysiology, and treatment for drug-induced pigmentation. This activity will present an overview of the etiology, clinical findings, and potential therapeutic factors that are important for members of the interprofessional team in the treatment of patients with drug-induced pigmentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In regards to clinical findings, drug-induced pigmentation varies from other causes of pigmentation. The most crucial distinction is that when the treatment with the drug stops, the pigmentation also begins to fade. For example, with the discontinuation of paclitaxel, the pigmentation also resolves shortly thereafter. [5] The discoloration associated with drug-induced pigmentation also tends to have a slower occurrence, with gradually worsening over months to a year. Moreover, particular drugs may have specific patterns of pigmentation. For example, NSAIDS typically involve fixed eruptions, whereas psychotropics are known for presenting with a blue-gray appearance and are related to sun exposure. Certain drugs are also associated with nail pigmentation. For example, antimalarials are more likely to cause nail beds that have transversal bands, whereas cytotoxic drugs such as cisplatin are more likely to present with longitudinal pigmented bands. [3] See the table for an overview of the characteristic pigmentation findings associated with particular drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, if there is another drug that can substitute as therapy for the patient’s condition, then that should be a consideration. If that is not possible, an effective approach involves reducing the dosage of a drug. Some drugs such as amiodarone have a dose-dependent correlation with the amount of discoloration experienced. [3] In these cases, decreasing the intake of a drug can dramatically reduce the dyschromia present. Additionally, specific drug-induced pigmentation is avoidable by limiting sun exposure. These drugs include antimalarials, psychotropic, amiodarone, and tetracyclines. [3] In these cases, patients should receive counsel on proper outside wear, such as sunglasses and protective, covered clothing. If the suggestions mentioned above failed to work, there are also some topical and laser treatments available. However, the efficacy of these treatment methods remains elusive.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications and associated symptoms of the pigmentation depend on the specific drug. NSAIDs are known to cause fixed drug eruptions, most probably through a hapten-type interaction. Antimalarials, amiodarone, and antipsychotics involve blue to gray discoloration in various areas from the face to the lower extremities. Anticonvulsants can cause brown-gray discoloration that resembles melasma. The pigmentation can also deposit in other areas such as the nails (antimalarials and minocycline). Antipsychotics, antimalarials, and amiodarone can even lead to corneal pigmentation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced pigmentation is the diagnosis when the pigmentation is temporally associated with drug use, and other potential causes have been ruled out. Several drugs can cause pigmentation from antimalarials to antiretrovirals. Therefore, clinicians should be vigilant in examining a patient’s full medical history to determine the specific drug that appears to be causing the patient’s symptoms. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of drug-induced pigmentation is difficult to ascertain because of a lack of reported cases and a dearth of information from patients regarding their treatment. However, estimates are that about 20% of cases of pigmentation are thought to be drug-induced. No significant differences between gender, age, and racial groups have been noted, although individuals with darker skin may display more severe hyperpigmentation. [2] More prospective studies investigating this issue are needed. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of drug-induced pigmentation. Review the history and physical exam of a patient with drug-induced pigmentation. Summarize the treatment and management options available for drug-induced pigmentation. Outline interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance drug-induced pigmentation and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tretinoin -- Indications -- FDA Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Topical tretinoin gel and cream FDA approved for: Topical application for treatment of acne vulgaris [1] Adjunctive palliative treatment of photoaging: Fine facial wrinkles [2] Facial skin roughness [2] Facial mottled hyperpigmentation (i.e., 'liver spots') [2] Oral tretinoin is FDA approved for the following: In acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), patients with refractory disease for remission induction who have previously relapsed from anthracycline chemotherapy or those who have a contraindication to anthracycline-based therapy [3] Presence of translocations on chromosomes 15 and 17 t(15;17) demonstrating French-American-British (FAB) classification M3 (including the M3 variant) [4] Patients who are refractory to or have had a relapse from or have contraindications to anthracycline chemotherapy with the presence of the PML/RARa gene [5] [6] Moderate to severe and cystic acne [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Pigmentation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced pigmentation is a form of abnormal skin pigmentation that is caused by drugs through several different mechanisms. Several drugs have associations with pigmentation, including cytotoxic agents, analgesics, anticoagulants, antimicrobials, antiretrovirals, metals, and antiarrhythmic, etc. Various causes can contribute to the pigmentation that may involve an accumulation of melanin synthesis or even the synthesis of particular substances. Histological findings are relatively diverse but can include substances that are primarily within the dermal macrophages. Diagnosing a patient with drug-induced pigmentation can be difficult, as it is essential to rule out other conditions that may be leading to the skin findings.

retrieved_docs.title: Cilia Incarnata -- Differential Diagnosis -- Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment, when needed, is similar to the treatments outlined under trichiasis if there is associated trichiasis or the increased hair causes irritation of the eye.

retrieved_docs.title: Diseases of the Eyelashes -- Etiology -- Hypotrichosis
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs: Systemic drug toxicity and local topical agents may cause madarosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Laugier-Hunziker Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders include lichen planus, Smoker’s melanosis, Benign racial pigmentation, melanonychia striata, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, Nutritional deficiency (vitamin B12 and folate), and heavy metal poisoning (lead, arsenic, mercury, gold, bismuth, and silver). [17] [18] [19] Generalized hypermelanosis can be seen with minocycline, phenothiazine, antimalarials, zidovudine, amiodarone, oral contraceptives, clofazimine, and chemotherapeutic agents. Disorders that display the pseudo-Hutchinson sign include Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, subungual hematoma, Bowen disease, and AIDS, while the true Hutchinson sign is specific to melanoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 43
retrieved_docs.content: in patients with thrombophlebitis, including its ocular manifestation retinal vein thrombophlebitis. These interrelated classifications illustrate how natural products, synthetic agents, and combination formulations are organized within pharmacological hierarchies, chemical families, and therapeutic categories, informing drug classification, contraindication management, and formulation design in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2797
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole and triazole derivatives, topical antifungals is a pharmacologic class that includes the antifungal agents fenticonazole, flutrimazole, and oxiconazole. All three drugs are members of this class and share the characteristic imidazole or triazole chemical structure used in topical treatment of fungal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: including parasitic.” Oxytetracycline is closely related to meclocycline, another topical tetracycline used for acne. Meclocycline and sulfacetamid are specifically grouped in the acne‑specific anti‑infective category. Clinical considerations for sulfonamides emphasize the risk of poisoning, adverse effects and underdosing in both gynecological and topical preparations. Overall, the knowledge base illustrates the interconnected taxonomy of antibacterial and anti‑infective agents, their formulation categories (gynecologic, ophthalmologic, topical), combination products, and therapeutic roles in skin, mucosal and systemic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1639
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatological Agents [TC] is a therapeutic category that includes distinct therapeutic classes such as haloprogin and selenium sulfide. Both haloprogin and selenium sulfide are identified as therapeutic classes within Dermatological Agents [TC], reflecting the variety of agents used to manage skin disorders under this broader dermatological classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2334
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoids for topical use in acne constitute a therapeutic class that includes both isotretinoin and tretinoin, with each of these agents being members of this specific group of acne‑treating retinoids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 104
retrieved_docs.content: anti‑acne topical agents, dermatologic antibiotics and chemotherapeutics, sulfonamide subcategories (ophthalmic, topical, gynecological) and the broader antibacterial class provide clinicians with a structured framework for therapy selection, recognition of cross‑category drug uses and anticipation of potential adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin, a systemic retinoid medication, is contraindicated in patients who have a liver disorder (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد), as the drug can cause hepatotoxic effects and exacerbate hepatic dysfunction; therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing acitretin to individuals with existing liver disease to ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Preparations containing sulfur that are used as anti‑acne topical agents are classified as members of the mesulfen group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1805
retrieved_docs.content: Klindamycin is classified as a member of the antiinfectives used for the treatment of acne.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662
retrieved_docs.content: ANTI-ACNE PREPARATIONS constitute a broad class of medications intended to treat acne, and anti‑acne preparations for topical use represent a specific subset of this class that are applied directly to the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives for treatment of acne constitute a therapeutic class that includes both erythromycin and sulfacetamid, each identified as a member of this anti‑infective group used specifically to manage acne.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1649
retrieved_docs.content: Natamycin is classified as an ophthalmological antifungal agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1354
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin and meclocycline are antiinfective agents that belong to the therapeutic class “Antiinfectives for treatment of acne.” As members of this anti‑acne medication category, both antibiotics are used to target the bacterial pathogens involved in acne vulgaris, thereby contributing to the management of this common dermatologic condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Haloprogin is categorized under the therapeutic class of Dermatological Agents, indicating that it is a medication specifically used for treating skin‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1815
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives for treatment of acne are classified as members of the sulfacetamide group, indicating that sulfacetamide—a sulfonamide antibiotic commonly used in topical formulations—constitutes the pharmacologic class for these acne‑directed antiinfective agents.
